title: Pyrography textures: fur, feather, and bark
subtitle: Burning techniques for three natural textures used in representational pyrography
excerpt: A technique entry covering three natural surface textures in pyrography: short-stroke fur, barbed feather, and rough bark. Each texture requires a different tip, temperature setting, and stroke pattern. Mastering these three opens most subjects in animal and nature pyrography.
sourceNotes: Victorian pokerwork manuals for decorative surface techniques; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

glossaryTerms.definition: The art of decorating wood by burning designs with a heated tip.
Shading technique using parallel lines of varying density. In pyrography, hatching is applied with the writing tip: closer lines produce darker values, wider spaced lines produce lighter ones.
Shading technique using dots rather than continuous strokes. Dot density controls tone: clustered dots for dark areas, sparse dots for light mid-tones.

content.text: Return the burner to its cooling stand between every stroke. Never rest the burner on the bench surface or on fabric.
All three textures below are worked in
by combining the same principles: tip shape, temperature, and stroke speed. Practise each texture on a scrap birch panel before applying to a finished piece.

Use the writing tip at medium-high temperature. Each fur stroke is a short curved line 5 to 15 mm long, made by resting the tip on the surface and pulling it toward the body in the direction of hair growth, lifting the tip at the end so the line tapers to a point. Work in layers: apply the darkest layer first across the full shadow area, then a lighter layer in the mid-tones, leaving the lightest areas with only one or two sparse strokes.

Use the writing tip at low-medium temperature. A feather barb is a shorter stroke than a fur stroke: 3 to 8 mm, angled at 45 degrees from the central quill line and lifting at the end. Apply barbs from the quill outward on both sides simultaneously, working from the tip of the feather toward the base. Use
across the base of each feather to suggest the denser downy covering.
Bark texture uses the shading tip at medium temperature for broad tonal areas, with

from the writing tip for the rough pitted surface. Start with the shading tip to establish the general light and dark areas of the bark pattern. Then work over the dark areas with the writing tip: short parallel strokes interrupted by occasional gaps to suggest fissures. Cluster dense stippled dots along the fissure edges to deepen them.

In a complete composition, the three textures sit at different distances: fur and feather read well at viewing distance; bark reads at arm's length. Keep the texture scale consistent within a subject. Where two textures meet, such as fur against bark, establish the boundary cleanly with the writing tip before filling each area.

items.symptom: Fur strokes look like parallel lines rather than hair
items.cause: All strokes made at the same angle and same length, without variation in curve or direction
items.fix: Vary the stroke direction within each region by 15 to 20 degrees from stroke to stroke, matching the direction the hair would naturally grow. Vary stroke length between 5 and 15 mm. The irregularity reads as natural hair; regularity reads as a ruled texture.
